does: This survey comprehensively examines trustworthiness in agentic AI systems—LLMs equipped with planning, tool use, memory, and extended interaction capabilities—by focusing on safety, robustness, privacy, and system security. The work identifies failure modes specific to multi-step agentic trajectories and maps risks across the full agent workflow from task decomposition through tool execution and long-horizon decision-making. It consolidates mitigation strategies at each stage, from input validation and output filtering to memory protection and tool sandboxing, while establishing a unified framework for evaluation metrics and benchmarks that captures both outcome signals (constraint violations, success rates) and process signals (trace completeness, intermediate step validity). The survey emphasizes scenario-to-metric guidance for deployment decisions and release gating in high-risk applications.
connects: This work builds on extensive prior research spanning adversarial robustness in neural networks, safety frameworks for autonomous systems, privacy-preserving machine learning, and recent work on LLM alignment and agent safety. It synthesizes insights from studies examining prompt injection, jailbreaking, and distribution shift vulnerabilities while incorporating established privacy techniques like differential privacy and federated learning adapted for agentic contexts. The survey responds to growing concern about emergent failure modes in multi-step reasoning systems that extend beyond single-turn language model safety, drawing on work in formal verification, sandboxing, and tool-use safety. It connects to broader trustworthiness literature covering interpretability, fairness, and accountability while prioritizing the dimensions most relevant to autonomous agent deployment.
enables: This structured framework enables practitioners to systematically assess and mitigate risks before deploying agentic systems in high-stakes domains including healthcare, finance, and critical infrastructure. The unified metrics-and-benchmarks hub supports reproducible evaluation and comparison across different agent architectures and deployment scenarios, facilitating evidence-based decisions about safety thresholds. By clarifying stage-specific vulnerabilities and mitigations, the survey enables development of more targeted defense mechanisms—better input validators, robust planning verifiers, and secure tool-execution environments. Future work can build specialized evaluation suites for particular domains, develop formal guarantees for agent behavior under distribution shift, and design compositional safety assurances that scale across complex multi-agent systems while maintaining privacy and interpretability.
